name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ إِن يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَيَأْتِ بِخَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ (١٩)
```

```english
Have you not regarded that Allah created the heavens and the earth with justice? If He wishes, He will take you away, and bring about a new creation, (19)
```

```arabic
وَمَا ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ بِعَزِيزٍ (٢٠)
```

```english
and that is not a formidable thing for Allah. (20)
```

```arabic
وَبَرَزُوا۟ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا فَقَالَ ٱلضُّعَفَـٰٓؤُا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُنَّا لَكُمْ تَبَعًا فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّغْنُونَ عَنَّا مِنْ عَذَابِ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ قَالُوا۟ لَوْ هَدَىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ لَهَدَيْنَـٰكُمْ ۖ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْنَآ أَجَزِعْنَآ أَمْ صَبَرْنَا مَا لَنَا مِن مَّحِيصٍ (٢١)
```

```english
Together they will be presented before Allah. Then those who were weak will say to the arrogant [leaders], ‘Indeed we were your followers. So will you avail us against Allah’s punishment in any wise?’ They will say, ‘Had Allah guided us, surely we would have guided you. It is the same to us whether we are restless or patient: there is no escape for us.’ (21)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لَمَّا قُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ وَعَدَكُمْ وَعْدَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَوَعَدتُّكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُكُمْ ۖ وَمَا كَانَ لِىَ عَلَيْكُم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍ إِلَّآ أَن دَعَوْتُكُمْ فَٱسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِى ۖ فَلَا تَلُومُونِى وَلُومُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُم ۖ مَّآ أَنَا۠ بِمُصْرِخِكُمْ وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُصْرِخِىَّ ۖ إِنِّى كَفَرْتُ بِمَآ أَشْرَكْتُمُونِ مِن قَبْلُ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٢٢)
```

```english
When the matter is all over, Satan will say, ‘Indeed Allah made you a promise that was true and I [too] made you a promise, but I failed you. I had no authority over you, except that I called you and you responded to me. So do not blame me, but blame yourselves. I cannot respond to your distress calls, neither can you respond to my distress calls. Indeed I disavow your taking me for [Allah’s] partner aforetime. There is indeed a painful punishment for the wrongdoers.’ (22)
```

```arabic
وَأُدْخِلَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ ۖ تَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيهَا سَلَـٰمٌ (٢٣)
```

```english
Those who have faith and do righteous deeds will be admitted into gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], by the leave of their Lord. Their greeting therein will be ‘Peace!’ (23)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ ضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ أَصْلُهَا ثَابِتٌ وَفَرْعُهَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ (٢٤)
```

```english
Have you not regarded how Allah has drawn a parable? A good word is like a good tree: its roots are steady and its branches are in the sky. (24)
```

```arabic
تُؤْتِىٓ أُكُلَهَا كُلَّ حِينٍۭ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهَا ۗ وَيَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٥)
```

```english
It gives its fruit every season by the leave of its Lord. Allah draws these parables for mankind so that they may take admonition. (25)
```

```arabic
وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ ٱجْتُثَّتْ مِن فَوْقِ ٱلْأَرْضِ مَا لَهَا مِن قَرَارٍ (٢٦)
```

```english
And the parable of a bad word is that of a bad tree: uprooted from the ground, it has no stability. (26)
```

```arabic
يُثَبِّتُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱلْقَوْلِ ٱلثَّابِتِ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَيُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ ۚ وَيَفْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ مَا يَشَآءُ (٢٧)
```

```english
Allah fortifies those who have faith with a constant creed in the life of this world and in the Hereafter, and Allah leads astray the wrongdoers, and Allah does whatever He wishes. (27)
```

```arabic
۞ أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ كُفْرًا وَأَحَلُّوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ دَارَ ٱلْبَوَارِ (٢٨)
```

```english
Have you not regarded those who have changed Allah’s blessing with ingratitude, and landed their people in the house of ruin? (28)
```

```arabic
جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَهَا ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْقَرَارُ (٢٩)
```

```english
—hell, which they shall enter, and it is an evil abode! (29)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ أَندَادًا لِّيُضِلُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۗ قُلْ تَمَتَّعُوا۟ فَإِنَّ مَصِيرَكُمْ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ (٣٠)
```

```english
They have set up equals to Allah, to lead [people] astray from His way. Say, ‘Enjoy [for a while], for indeed your destination is hellfire!’ (30)
```

```arabic
قُل لِّعِبَادِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ سِرًّا وَعَلَانِيَةً مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ يَوْمٌ لَّا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَلَا خِلَـٰلٌ (٣١)
```

```english
Tell My servants who have faith to maintain the prayer and to spend out of what We have provided them with, secretly and openly, before there comes a day on which there will be neither any bargaining nor friendship. (31)
```

```arabic
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَأَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَخْرَجَ بِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ رِزْقًا لَّكُمْ ۖ وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلْفُلْكَ لِتَجْرِىَ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِۦ ۖ وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرَ (٣٢)
```

```english
It is Allah who created the heavens and the earth, and He sends down water from the sky and with it He brings forth crops for your sustenance. And He disposed the ships for you[r benefit] so that they may sail at sea by His command, and He disposed the rivers for you. (32)
```

```arabic
وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ دَآئِبَيْنِ ۖ وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ (٣٣)
```

```english
He disposed the sun and the moon for you, constant [in their courses], and He disposed the night and the day, (33)
```

```arabic
وَءَاتَىٰكُم مِّن كُلِّ مَا سَأَلْتُمُوهُ ۚ وَإِن تَعُدُّوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ لَا تُحْصُوهَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ (٣٤)
```

```english
and He gave you all that you had asked Him. If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them. Indeed man is most unfair and ungrateful! (34)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses comprise a reminder to the people, presented in the form of repeated addresses to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) through His sayings: **أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِ** — "Have you not regarded that Allah created the heavens and the earth with truth?" (14:19); **أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ ضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً** — "Have you not regarded how Allah draws a parable?" (14:24); and **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً** — "Have you not regarded those who substituted ungratefulness for Allah’s blessing?" (14:28).

Through these, the Exalted mentions that creation is founded upon truth (الحق); thus, all shall be brought forth. Those who walked by the truth, believed in the truth, and acted according to the truth shall attain felicity (السعادة) and Paradise (الجنة). Conversely, those who followed falsehood (الباطل), worshipped Satan (الشيطان), and obeyed the arrogant tyrants (الطغاة المستكبرين) out of delusion (غروراً) by the outward appearance of their might and power, shall be bound by an inescapable wretchedness (شقاء). Their leaders, from among the jinn and humans, shall disown them, while to Allah belongs all might and praise.

He then mentions that this division into two groups is due to the division of their conduct into two paths: the path of guidance (الهدى) and the path of misguidance (الضلال). He who is bound by guidance is the believer (المؤمن), and he who is bound by misguidance is the wrongdoer (الظالم). The Judge in this matter is Allah, the Glorified; He does what He wills, and to Him belongs all might and praise.

Furthermore, He reminds of the past perished nations and the ruin (البوار) they fell into because of their ingratitude toward the blessing of Allah, the All-Mighty, the All-Laudable (العزيز الحميد). He reproaches man for his wrongdoing (ظلم) and his disbelief (كفر) in the divine blessings that fill existence—"and if you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them."

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِ** — "Have you not regarded that Allah created the heavens and the earth with truth?" (14:19). What is meant by "regarding" (الرؤية) here is definitive knowledge (العلم القاطع), for it is this that is suitable to relate to the modality of the creation of the heavens and the earth, rather than visual perception.

The "truthful act" (الفعل الحق)—the opposite of which is "falsehood" (الباطل)—is that for which the agent has a desired goal (غاية مطلوبة) toward which he moves by his very essence. It is observed that every species, from the beginning of its formation, is directed toward a deferred goal; it has no objective other than reaching it. Furthermore, some species serve as a goal for others, benefiting them in the course of their becoming and suiting them in their emergence and survival—such as the terrestrial elements utilized by plants, and plants utilized by animals, and so on. The Exalted said: **وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ ما خَلَقْناهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** — "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with truth, but most of them do not know" (44:38-39). And He said: **وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما باطِلاً ذلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا** — "We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is the opinion of those who are faithless" (38:27).

Thus, creation continues to occur stage after stage, attaining goal after goal, until it halts at a final goal beyond which there is no other—and that is its return to Allah, the Glorified. The Exalted said: **وَأَنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى** — "and that the terminus is toward your Lord" (53:42).

In sum, an act is only a "truthful act" (فعلاً حقاً) if there is a matter intended by the agent through his act and a goal toward which he proceeds by that act. However, if it is an act by which nothing is intended except the act itself, without there being a desired purpose (غرض مطلوب), then it is a "false act" (الفعل الباطل). If a false act possesses a system and order, it is called "play" (لعباً), just as children play by performing organized and ordered movements without having any goal beyond them, nor any concern other than bringing into existence what they imagined of the form of the act due to a psychological desire for it.

His act, the Exalted, is inseparable from the truth (الحق) and accompanied by it. Thus, the creation of the heavens and the earth leaves behind an enduring world after its passing. Were it not so, it would be falsehood (باطلاً) with no trace or successor to follow it, and the observed world, with its wondrous order, would be "play" (لعباً) on the part of the Glorified, undertaken due to a need for it—such as breathing to relieve distress or boredom, seeking diversion from anxiety, or escaping the loneliness of solitude and the like. Yet He, the Glorified, is the All-Mighty, the All-Laudable (العزيز الحميد); no need touches Him, nor does poverty or want abase Him.

From what has passed, it becomes clear that the preposition "with" (بـ) in His saying **بِالْحَقِ** (with truth) denotes accompaniment (المصاحبة). The view of some—that the preposition denotes causality (السببية) or instrumentality (الآلة), meaning He created them by His "truthful word," or that it denotes a "truthful purpose"—is not as it should be.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَيَأْتِ بِخَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ وَما ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ بِعَزِيزٍ** — "If He wishes, He will take you away, and bring a new creation, and that is not difficult for Allah" (14:19-20). That is, it is not arduous or hard. The address is to humanity in general, using the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) as an exemplar for them, because the address is directed to him in the preceding and succeeding verses: **أَلَمْ تَرَ** (Have you not regarded) and **وَما ذلِكَ** (and that is not).

It has been previously established that creation being "with truth" (بالحق) is a requirement of His being, the Exalted, All-Mighty (عزيزاً) and Self-Sufficient in His Essence (غنياً بالذات). For if His Self-Sufficiency did not necessitate this, and if it were possible for "play" (اللعب) to proceed from Him, the Exalted, then this observed creation—with its wondrous order—would be play intended for nothing but emergence and annihilation. This would stem from an imaginary desire on His part and an internal need, such as venting distress, relieving anxiety, or seeking company against loneliness and boredom, and the like. However, His Essential Self-Sufficiency (غناه بالذات) repels such a notion.

Perhaps this subtle point is what necessitated following His saying **أَنَّ اللهَ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ** (that Allah created the heavens and the earth) with His saying **إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ** (If He wishes, He will take you away), etc. Thus, His saying **إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ** serves as an exposition (بيان) for what preceded it. The meaning is: Do you not know that Allah created this observed creation out of His Might and Self-Sufficiency, and that if He wishes, He will take you away and bring a new creation? That is not difficult for Him, the Exalted, for He is Allah—exalted is His Name (عز اسمه)—to Whom belong the Most Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) and all might and majesty.

By this, it becomes clear that using the manifest noun (الظاهر) in place of the pronoun (المضمر) in His saying **عَلَى اللهِ** (for Allah) is to indicate the proof (الحجة): that the lack of difficulty in this matter for Him, the Exalted, is by virtue of Him being Allah, exalted is His Name.

If you were to say: If the purpose of bringing the phrase **إِنْ يَشَأْ** (If He wishes), etc., was to indicate His absolute Self-Sufficiency and that He is not "playing" with creation, it would have been more appropriate to limit it to His saying **إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ** (If He wishes, He will take you away) and omit the phrase **وَيَأْتِ بِخَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ** (and bring a new creation). For taking away the old and bringing the new does not negate "play," as the very act of taking some away and bringing others could itself be play.

I say: This would be so if it were said: "If He wishes, He will take away all of creation and bring a new creation." However, since it was said **إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ** (If He wishes, He will take you away), and the address is to humanity in general—or to the nation of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) specifically, or to those present in his era—it was necessary to follow it with His saying **وَيَأْتِ بِخَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ** (and bring a new creation). For this observed creation, with the interconnection and dependence between its parts, cannot fulfill its purpose except through this existing quality and specific composition and harmony. If He were to take away humans while leaving the heavens and the earth in their current state, that would be falsehood (باطلاً) and play (لعباً) from another perspective.

In other words, taking away humans alone without bringing a new creation, while maintaining the rest of the observed creation, would be a false play. Similarly, taking away creation from its root without a subsequent goal would be a false play. Rather, the "truth" (الحق) that reveals His Self-Sufficiency, the Exalted, is that He takes away one people and brings others—and this is what the noble verse mentions. Understand this well.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَبَرَزُوا لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً** — "They will all emerge before Allah" (14:21) to the end of the verse. "Emergence" (البروز) is to go out into the open (البراز)—with a *fatha* on the *ba*—which is an open space. It is said: "he emerged to him" (برز إليه) when he went out to him such that no barrier (حاجب) hides him from him. From this comes "dueling" (المبارزة) and "combat" (البراز), like the exit of a fighter from the rank to face his peer from the enemy.

The term "followers" (التبع)—with two *fathas*—is the plural of "follower" (تابع), like "servants" (خدم) is the plural of "servant" (خادم). It is also said to be a collective noun (اسم جمع), or a verbal noun (مصدر) used for emphasis (مبالغة). "Availing" (الإغناء) means providing benefit, and it here carries the sense of "repelling" (الدفع), which is why it is made transitive with the preposition "from" (عن), as has been said. "Impatience" (الجزع) and "patience" (الصبر) are opposites. "Escape" (المحيص) is a noun of place from "he swerved" (حاص يحيص), meaning to move away from something disliked, as stated in *al-Majma'*. Thus, the "escape" (المحيص) is the place to which a person moves to avoid dislike and hardship.

His saying **وَبَرَزُوا لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً** (They will all emerge before Allah) means they appeared before Him, the Exalted, an appearance where no barrier hides them from Him. This is in relation to themselves, as they used to imagine in the worldly life (الدنبا) that their Lord was absent from them and they were absent from Him. When the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) comes, every imagined veil shall vanish, and they shall witness that there is no barrier there to hide them from Him. As for Him, the Exalted, no cover hides anything from Him in this world or the hereafter. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَخْفى عَلَيْهِ شَيْءٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا فِي السَّماءِ** — "Nothing is indeed hidden from Allah in the earth or in the sky" (3:5).

It is possible that the sentence is a metaphor (كناية) for their being singled out for the reckoning of deeds (حساب الأعمال) and the attachment of the Divine Will to the cessation of actions and the fulfillment of the promised recompense, as He said: **سَنَفْرُغُ لَكُمْ أَيُّهَ الثَّقَلانِ** — "Soon We shall attend to you, O you two heavy weights!" (55:31).

His saying **فَقالَ الضُّعَفاءُ لِلَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا** (Then the weak will say to those who were arrogant) until His saying **مِنْ شَيْءٍ** (in any way) describes a dispute between the disbelievers on the Day of Resurrection—as the context suggests. The "weak" (الضعفاء) are the imitators (المقلدون) who were obedient to their masters among the disbelievers. The "arrogant" (المستكبرون) are their followed masters, the possessors of influence and power who disdained faith in Allah and His signs.

The meaning is: the weak imitators said to those who were arrogant among them: "In the worldly life, we were followers and obedient to you, without asking you for proof for what you commanded us. So are you providing us any benefit today by repelling from us anything of the punishment of Allah that has been decreed upon us?"

Accordingly, the word "of" (من) in His saying **مِنْ عَذابِ اللهِ** (of the punishment of Allah) is for exposition (للبيان), and in His saying **مِنْ شَيْءٍ** (in any way) it is extra for emphasis (للتأكيد), as in our saying: "No one (من أحد) came to me." Negation and interrogation are similar in their grammatical ruling, and there is no evidence for the impossibility of the exposition (البيان) preceding the thing expounded (المبين), especially when they are connected without separation.

His saying **قالُوا لَوْ هَدانَا اللهُ لَهَدَيْناكُمْ** (They will say, ‘Had Allah guided us, we would have surely guided you’)—the apparent context is that "guidance" (الهداية) here refers to guidance to the way of escaping the punishment. It is also possible that it refers to guidance to the true religion (الدين الحق) in the worldly life. The ultimate result is the same due to the correspondence between the worldly life and the hereafter; nothing emerges in the latter except what was latent in the former. The Exalted said, narrating from the people of Paradise: **وَقالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي هَدانا لِهذا وَما كُنَّا لِهتَدِيَ لَوْ لا أَنْ هَدانَا اللهُ لَقَدْ جاءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنا بِالْحَقِّ** — "and they will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who guided us to this. We would have never been guided had not Allah guided us. Our Lord’s apostles certainly brought the truth’" (7:43). They blended the two guidances together, as is apparent.

His saying **سَواءٌ عَلَيْنا أَجَزِعْنا أَمْ صَبَرْنا ما لَنا مِنْ مَحِيصٍ** (It is the same to us whether we are restless or patient: there is no escape for us)—"same" (سواء), "equality" (الاستواء), and "parity" (التساوي) are one. "Same" (سواء) is the predicate of a deleted subject, and the interrogative sentence is an exposition of that. His saying **ما لَنا مِنْ مَحِيصٍ** (there is no escape for us) is another exposition of the parity. The meaning is: the two matters are equal for us and in relation to us—namely, restlessness and patience—there is no place of flight for us from the inescapable punishment.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَقالَ الشَّيْطانُ لَمَّا قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ** — "When the matter is decided, Satan will say..." (14:22) to the end of the verse. In *al-Majma'*: "Succoring" (الإصراخ) is providing aid by responding to the one crying for help (الصارخ). It is said: "So-and-so cried out to me (استصرخني) and I succored him (فأصرخته)," meaning he sought my aid and I aided him. End quote.

This is a comprehensive speech delivered by Satan on the Day of Resurrection to the wrongdoers (الظالمين), in which he clarifies his position regarding them and informs the gathered people of the true nature of the link that existed between him and them in the worldly life. Allah, the Glorified, has promised that He will inform them on the Day of Resurrection of that wherein they used to differ, and that the truth will appear on that Day from the side of everyone for whom it was hidden or ambiguous. Thus, the angels shall disown their polytheism (شركهم), the jinn and the "associates" (القرناء) from among the devils shall cast them away, the idols and deities they took as lords besides Allah shall disbelieve in their polytheism, their leaders and imams of misguidance shall not respond to them, and the guilty themselves shall confess to their misguidance and crime. All of this occurs in many verses that are not hidden from the observant and contemplative seeker.

Although "Satan" (الشيطان) can mean any "evil one" (الشرير) and is sometimes applied in the speech of the Exalted to every evil one among jinn and humans—as in His saying: **وَكَذلِكَ جَعَلْنا لِكُلِّ نَبِيٍّ عَدُوًّا شَياطِينَ الْإِنْسِ وَالْجِنِّ** — "That is how for every prophet We appointed as enemy the devils from among humans and jinn" (6:112)—what is meant by it in this verse is the Satan who is the source of all seduction and misguidance for the children of Adam, namely Iblis (إبليس). For the apparent context is that he addresses with this speech the generality of the wrongdoers among the gathered people and confesses that he used to call them to polytheism. The Quran has explicitly stated that the one who holds this position is Iblis; he claimed this for himself and Allah did not refute him, as in His saying: **قالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ** — "He said, ‘By Your might, I will surely pervert them all, except Your dedicated servants among them’" (38:82-83) until He said: **لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكَ وَمِمَّنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** — "I will surely fill hell with you and with those of them who follow you, all together" (38:85).

As for his progeny and his tribe, whom the Quran mentions in His saying: **إِنَّهُ يَراكُمْ هُوَ وَقَبِيلُهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا تَرَوْنَهُمْ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا الشَّياطِينَ أَوْلِياءَ لِلَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — "Indeed he and his tribe see you from where you do not see them. We have indeed made the devils friends of those who do not have faith" (7:27) and His saying: **أَفَتَتَّخِذُونَهُ وَذُرِّيَّتَهُ أَوْلِياءَ** — "Will you then take him and his offspring for friends?" (18:50)—the guardianship (ولاية) of any one of them is either for some people and not others, or in some deeds and not others. As for guardianship in the sense of assistance, that is "aid" (العونية). The root to which the matter of misguidance and seduction returns is Iblis.

Thus, this speaker—**إِنَّ اللهَ وَعَدَكُمْ وَعْدَ الْحَقِ** (Indeed Allah made you a true promise)—is Iblis. He intends by his speech to redirect the blame for committing sins back to them and to disown their polytheism. His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ وَعَدَكُمْ وَعْدَ الْحَقِّ وَوَعَدْتُكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُكُمْ** (Indeed Allah made you a true promise, and I [too] made you promises, but I failed you) means: Allah made you a promise that occurrence has realized and witnessing has confirmed—concerning the Resurrection, the Gathering, the Reckoning, the Decisive Judgment, Paradise, and the Fire. "And I promised you that there would be no Resurrection, no Reckoning, no Paradise, and no Fire, and I did not fulfill what I promised, as the opposite of what I promised has appeared." This is how the commentators have mentioned it.

Accordingly, the "promised" (الموعود) includes everything related to the Return (المعاد), both in affirmation and negation—affirmed by Allah, the Glorified, and negated by Iblis. "Failing the promise" (إخلاف الوعد) is a metaphor for the appearance of the lie and the non-occurrence, by mentioning the cause and intending the effect.

It is possible—indeed, it is the preferred view—that the promise includes what results from faith and polytheism in both the worldly life and the hereafter, for they are corresponding. Allah promised the people of faith a "good life" (حياة طيبة) and a happy existence, and He promised the people of polytheism who turn away from His remembrance a "distressed life" (معيشة ضنكاً), constriction in their breasts, and punishment in their hearts in the worldly life. He promised everyone Resurrection, Reckoning, Paradise, and the Fire in the hereafter.

Iblis promised his followers delightful passions and long hopes; he made them forget death, diverted them from the Resurrection and Reckoning, and made them fear poverty, humiliation, and the blame of people. His key in all of this was making them heedless of the station of their Lord, beautifying the worldly causes before them as being independent in their influence and creators of their own effects, and portraying their own selves to them in the form of independence, dominating all other causes and managing them as they pleased. Thus, he enticed them to rely on themselves instead of Allah and to subjugate causes in the path of hopes and desires.

In sum, Allah promised them regarding the worldly life and the hereafter that which He fulfilled for them. Iblis called them through heedlessness and beautification toward illusions and desires—which are either things man will certainly never attain, or things which, if he attains them, he finds to be other than what he imagined, so he leaves them for what he [next] imagines. This is in the worldly life. As for the hereafter, he makes them forget its affairs, as previously mentioned.

His saying: **وَما كانَ لِي عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ إِلَّا أَنْ دَعَوْتُكُمْ فَاسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِي** — "I had no authority over you, except that I called you and you responded to me" (14:22). "Authority" (السلطان)—as mentioned by al-Raghib—is "dominion" (السلاطة), which is the capacity for coercion (القهر). Proof (الحجة) is also called "authority" because of its capacity to coerce intellects toward its conclusions. It is often used to refer to the possessor of authority, such as a king or others.

The apparent meaning is what is more general than physical or conceptual authority. The meaning is: "In the worldly life, I had no dominion over you, neither from the side of your persons and essences—such that I could force you into disobedience to Allah by stripping you of your choice and imposing my will upon you—nor from the side of your intellects—such that I could establish for you a proof for polytheism however I wished, so that your intellects would be compelled to accept it and your souls would obey them in what they command."

It is also apparent that the exception in His saying **إِلَّا أَنْ دَعَوْتُكُمْ** (except that I called you) is "disconnected" (منقطعاً). The meaning is: "But I called you without any authority, and you responded to me." His calling people to polytheism and disobedience, though it was by Allah's permission, was not an empowerment (تسليطاً). For calling toward an act is not a dominion of the caller over the act of the one called, even if it is a type of dominion over the call itself. Evidence for this is the saying of the Exalted regarding what He permits him: **وَاسْتَفْزِزْ مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتَ مِنْهُمْ بِصَوْتِكَ** — "And unsettle whomever of them you can with your voice" (17:64) until He said: **وَعِدْهُمْ وَما يَعِدُهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ إِلَّا غُرُوراً إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ وَكَفى بِرَبِّكَ وَكِيلاً** — "and make them promises. But Satan does not promise them anything but deceit. As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, and your Lord suffices as a guardian" (17:64-65).

From here, the invalidity of what al-Razi argued in his *Tafsir* regarding the exception being "connected" (متصلاً) becomes clear. He said: "The power to move a person toward something is sometimes by coercion from the mover, and sometimes by strengthening the motive in his heart, and that is by casting whispers (الوسواس) to him. This is a type of dominion. So it is as if he said: 'I had no dominion over you except by whispering, not by striking and the like.'"

The reason for its invalidity is that the fact that a mere call is not "authority" (سلطاناً) or a capacity for coercion over the one called is self-evident and does not accept doubt. Thus, counting it as a type of dominion is something not to be heeded.

Yes, a psychological inclination toward the thing called to may arise from the one called, so he submits to the call, and by his response, he grants the caller dominion over himself. However, this is an "empowerment" (تسليط) from the one called, not an "authority" (تسلط) from the caller. In other words, it is an authority that the one called possesses over himself and then grants to the caller; it is not that the caller possesses it over him by himself. Iblis only negates the authority that he would possess from himself, not what they empower him with over themselves by their submission, as evidenced by his saying: **فَلا تَلُومُونِي وَلُومُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ** (so do not blame me, but blame yourselves).

This is the "authority" (التسلط) that Allah, the Glorified, affirms for him in His saying: **إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ لَهُ سُلْطانٌ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَلى رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ إِنَّما سُلْطانُهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُ وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِهِ مُشْرِكُونَ** — "Indeed he has no authority over those who have faith and put their trust in their Lord. His authority is only over those who befriend him and those who make him a partner [of Allah]" (16:99-100) or His saying: **إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ** — "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you" (15:42). The verses—as you see—are apparent in that his authority is a branch of following, befriending, and polytheism, not the reverse.

Due to the total negation of his authority over them, he concluded with his subsequent saying: **فَلا تَلُومُونِي وَلُومُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ** (so do not blame me, but blame yourselves). The "fa" (فـ) is for derivation (للتفريع), meaning: "Since I had no authority over you in any way—as indicated by the use of the indefinite noun in the context of negation and the emphasis with 'of' (من) in His saying **وَما كانَ لِي عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ**—then none of the blame returning to you from the side of polytheism and disobedience returns to me. It is not right for you to blame me; rather, it is your duty to blame yourselves because you possessed the authority over your own action."

His saying: **ما أَنَا بِمُصْرِخِكُمْ وَما أَنْتُمْ بِمُصْرِخِيَ** (I cannot succor you, nor can you succor me) means: "I am not your aider or savior, and you are not my aiders or saviors. So I am not an intercessor (شافع) for you, nor are you intercessors for me today."

His saying: **إِنِّي كَفَرْتُ بِما أَشْرَكْتُمُونِ مِنْ قَبْلُ** (Indeed I disavow your having taken me for a partner before) means: "I have disowned your associating me [with Allah] in the worldly life." What is meant by "associating" (الإشراك) is association in obedience (الطاعة), not association in worship (العبادة), as appears from the saying of the Exalted addressing the gathered people: **أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي** — "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, ‘Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me’" (36:60-61).

This speech from him is a disavowal of their polytheism, just as the Glorified narrated the disavowal of every false followed one from his follower on the Day of Resurrection. It is the revelation that their associating him with Allah in the worldly life was nothing but a mirage-like illusion. The Exalted said: **وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكْفُرُونَ بِشِرْكِكُمْ** — "and on the Day of Resurrection they will disavow your polytheism" (35:14). And He said: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوا لَوْ أَنَّ لَنا كَرَّةً فَنَتَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُمْ كَما تَبَرَّؤُا مِنَّا** — "and the followers will say, ‘Had there been another turn for us, we would disown them as they have disowned us’" (2:167). And He said: **قالَ الَّذِينَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ الَّذِينَ أَغْوَيْنا أَغْوَيْناهُمْ كَما غَوَيْنا تَبَرَّأْنا إِلَيْكَ ما كانُوا إِيَّانا يَعْبُدُونَ وَقِيلَ ادْعُوا شُرَكاءَكُمْ فَدَعَوْهُمْ فَلَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَهُمْ** — "Those against whom the word has become due will say, ‘Our Lord! These are the ones whom we perverted. We perverted them as we were perverted ourselves. We disavow them before You: it was not us that they worshipped.’ It will be said, ‘Invoke your partners!’ So they will invoke them, but they will not respond to them" (28:63-64).

His saying: **إِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ** (Indeed for the wrongdoers there is a painful punishment) is part of the speech of Iblis, as the context suggests. He records against them the painful punishment because they are wrongdoers whose wrongdoing returns only to themselves.

The apparent context is that His saying **ما أَنَا بِمُصْرِخِكُمْ وَما أَنْتُمْ بِمُصْرِخِيَ** (I cannot succor you, nor can you succor me) is a metaphor (كناية) for the negation of the link between him and his followers, as the Exalted points to in other places with sayings such as: **لَقَدْ تَقَطَّعَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْكُمْ ما كُنْتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ** — "The tie between you is now cut, and what you used to claim has forsaken you" (6:94) and His saying: **فَزَيَّلْنا بَيْنَهُمْ وَقالَ شُرَكاؤُهُمْ ما كُنْتُمْ إِيَّانا يَعْبُدُونَ** — "Then We shall set them apart from one another, and their partners will say, ‘It was not us that you worshipped’" (10:28).

This is because it is clear that if it were not a metaphor, his saying **وَما أَنْتُمْ بِمُصْرِخِيَ** (nor can you succor me) would be redundant and unnecessary, as no purpose attaches to it. Neither do they imagine that they are capable of succoring Iblis or interceding for him, nor does he imagine that, nor does the situation suggest it. Thus, he says: "Do not blame me, but blame yourselves," because the link is cut between me and you. It does not benefit you that I was your followed one, nor does it benefit me that you were my followers. I have disowned your polytheism, so I am not a partner to Him, the Exalted. I have only disowned it because you are wrongdoers against yourselves, and for the wrongdoers there is a painful punishment; there is no justification on that Day for protecting them or drawing near to them.

This context—as you see—testifies that the followers of Iblis blame him on the Day of Resurrection for the calamity that befell them due to following him, expecting him to share in their calamity in some way. He refutes them by stating that there is no link between him and them; thus, their blame attaches only to themselves. He cannot touch them or approach them because he fears the painful punishment prepared for the wrongdoers, and they are wrongdoers. Thus, it is close in meaning to the saying of the Exalted: **كَمَثَلِ الشَّيْطانِ إِذْ قالَ لِلْإِنْسانِ اكْفُرْ فَلَمَّا كَفَرَ قالَ إِنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِنْكَ إِنِّي أَخافُ اللهَ رَبَّ الْعالَمِينَ** — "Like Satan when he said to man, ‘Disbelieve.’ Then when he disbelieved, he said, ‘I disown you. Indeed I fear Allah, the Lord of all the worlds’" (59:16).

Perhaps it is from here that some said the meaning of His saying **إِنِّي كَفَرْتُ** (Indeed I disavowed), etc., refers to his disbelief in the worldly life, such that "before" (من قبل) relates only to His saying "I disavowed," or to both it and His saying "you took me for a partner" by way of grammatical competition (التنازع).

In sum, the primary objective in the verse is that man is the one responsible for his action because the authority (السلطان) belongs to him and not to another; so let him blame none but himself. As for the link of "following" and "being followed," it is illusory and has no reality. This reality will appear on the Day of Resurrection when Satan disowns him and returns the blame to him, just as was explained in the previous verse that the link between the weak and the arrogant is illusory and avails them nothing when the need for it arises on the Day of Resurrection, at the time of the uncovering of realities.

The commentators have various differing opinions on the segments of the verse which we have refrained from citing. Whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the extensive commentaries.

In the verse, there is a clear indication that man possesses authority over his action, which is what necessitates the connection of recompense to it and strips it from others, and it is what returns the blame to him and not to another. As for his being independent (مستقلاً) in this authority, there is no indication of that in the verse at all. We have discussed this in the first volume of the book under His saying: **وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ** — "and He does not mislead by it any except the transgressors" (2:26).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَأُدْخِلَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ جَنَّاتٍ** — "Those who have faith and do righteous deeds will be admitted into gardens..." (14:23), etc., is an exposition of the ultimate state of the felicitous among the believers. In His saying **تَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيها سَلامٌ** (their greeting therein will be ‘Peace!’), there is a contrast between their state—where peace and blessed greetings are reflected from one to another—and the state of the others mentioned in the two previous verses, characterized by dispute and confronting one another with disbelief, disavowal, and despair.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ ضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ أَصْلُها ثابِتٌ وَفَرْعُها فِي السَّماءِ تُؤْتِي أُكُلَها كُلَّ حِينٍ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّها** — "Have you not regarded how Allah draws a parable? A good word is like a good tree: its roots are steady and its branches are in the sky. It gives its fruit every season by the leave of its Lord" (14:24-25). They mentioned that "a word" (كلمة) is an "appositive of inclusion" (بدل اشتمال) for "a parable" (مثلاً), and "like a tree" (كشجرة) is an attribute after an attribute for His saying "a word," or the predicate of a deleted subject, the estimation being "it is like a tree." It is also said that "a word" is the first object of "draws" (ضرب), placed later, and "a parable" is its second object, placed earlier to avoid the problem of separation between "a word" and its attribute, which is "like a tree." The estimation would be: "Allah drew a good word like a good tree... as a parable."

It is also said that "draws" (ضرب) is transitive to one object, and "a word" is in the accusative case due to a hidden verb like "made" or "took." The estimation would be: "Allah drew a parable; He made a good word like a good tree," etc. I think this is the best of the views if it is explained as "a good word," etc., being an "explanatory appositive" (عطف بيان) for His saying "Allah draws a parable," as an exposition of one sentence by another. It then becomes necessary to put "a word" in the accusative by a hidden verb like "made" or "took," because the meaning is that He likened the word to the tree and compared it to it, which is the meaning of our saying: "He took a good word like a tree," etc.

His saying **أَصْلُها ثابِتٌ** (its roots are steady) means firmly established in the earth, striking its roots into it. His saying **وَفَرْعُها فِي السَّماءِ** (and its branches are in the sky) means that which branches out from that root of its limbs in the direction of height; for everything that is high and provides shade is "sky" (سماء). His saying **تُؤْتِي أُكُلَها كُلَّ حِينٍ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّها** (It gives its fruit every season by the leave of its Lord) means it produces its edible fruit in every time by the permission of Allah. This is the ultimate benefit a tree can provide of blessings.

They differed in the verse, firstly, regarding what is meant by the "good word" (الكلمة الطيبة). It was said: it is the testimony that there is no god but Allah; it was said: faith (الإيمان); it was said: the Quran; it was said: absolute glorification and hallowing (التسبيح والتنزيه); it was said: praise of Allah in general; it was said: every good word; it was said: all acts of obedience; and it was said: the believer.

Secondly, they differed regarding what is meant by the "good tree" (الشجرة الطيبة). It was said: the date palm—which is the view of the majority; it was said: the coconut tree; it was said: every tree that produces good fruit like the fig, the grape, and the pomegranate; and it was said: a tree whose description is what Allah described, even if it does not exist in actuality.

Then they differed regarding what is meant by "season" or "time" (الحين). It was said: two months; it was said: six months; it was said: a full year; it was said: every morning and evening; and it was said: all times.

Engaging in such disputes is among the things that divert man from what concerns him of investigating the knowledge of the Book of Allah and attaining the objectives and purposes of the noble verses.

What contemplation (التدبر) of the verses yields is that what is meant by the "good word" (الكلمة الطيبة)—which is likened to a good tree with such-and-such attributes—is the true, steady belief (الاعتقاد الحق الثابت). For He, the Exalted, says afterward—and it is like the conclusion drawn from the parable: **يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ** — "Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter" (14:27). The "saying" (القول) is the "word" (الكلمة), and not every word insofar as it is a mere utterance, but insofar as it relies upon a belief and a resolve upon which man remains steadfast and from which he does not deviate in action.

The Exalted has addressed a meaning close to this in several places of His speech, such as His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ قالُوا رَبُّنَا اللهُ ثُمَّ اسْتَقامُوا فَلا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهمْ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ** — "Indeed those who say, ‘Our Lord is Allah,’ and then remain steadfast, no fear shall on them, nor shall they grieve" (46:13); and His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ قالُوا رَبُّنَا اللهُ ثُمَّ اسْتَقامُوا تَتَنَزَّلُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ أَلَّا تَخافُوا وَلا تَحْزَنُوا** — "Indeed those who say, ‘Our Lord is Allah!’ and then remain steadfast, the angels descend upon them, [saying,] ‘Do not fear, nor be grieved’" (41:30); and His saying: **إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ** — "To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous deeds" (35:10).

This "saying" and "good word" is that upon which He, the Exalted, bases His fortification (تثبيته) of its people in the worldly life and the hereafter—and they are those who believed. Then He contrasts it with the misguiding of the wrongdoers, and contrasts it from another perspective with the state of the polytheists. By this, it becomes clear that what is meant by the "thing likened" (الممثل) is the word of monotheism (كلمة التوحيد) and the testimony that there is no god but Allah with a true testimony.

Thus, the saying of oneness and steadfastness upon it is the "true saying" (حق القول) which has a steady root (أصل ثابت) preserved from all change, vanishing, and invalidity—and that is Allah, exalted is His Name, or the land of realities. It has branches that have emerged and grown without any obstacle hindering them—namely, the true derivative beliefs, pure ethics, and righteous deeds by which the believer lives his "good life" and by which he populates the human world with its true population. These are what harmonize with the course of the cosmic order that led to the emergence of man with his existence fashioned upon true belief and righteous deed.

The perfect ones among the believers—those who said "Our Lord is Allah" and then remained steadfast, thus realizing this firm saying and good word—are like their saying upon which they remained steady: people continue to benefit from the goodness of their existence and are blessed by their graces.

Similarly, every true word and every righteous deed is like this parable: it has a steady root, upright branches, and good, beneficial fruits.

Thus, the parable mentioned in the verse applies to all, as supported by the expression "a good word" in the indefinite form. However, what is intended in the verse, according to the context, is the root of monotheism (أصل التوحيد) from which all other true beliefs branch out, upon which pure ethics grow, and from which righteous deeds emerge.

Then Allah, the Glorified, concluded the verse with His saying: **وَيَضْرِبُ اللهُ الْأَمْثالَ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ** — "Allah draws parables for mankind so that they may take admonition" (14:25), so that the one who takes admonition may remember that there is no escape for the seeker of felicity from realizing the word of monotheism and remaining steadfast upon it.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ اجْتُثَّتْ مِنْ فَوْقِ الْأَرْضِ ما لَها مِنْ قَرارٍ** — "And the parable of a bad word is that of a bad tree: uprooted from the ground, it has no stability" (14:26). "Uprooting" (الاجتثاث) is to pull out. It is said: "I pulled it out" (جثته واجتثته). The "mound" (الجث)—with a *damma*—is what is raised from the ground like a hillock, and the "body" (جثة) of a thing is its protruding figure. This is in *al-Mufradat*.

The "bad word" (الكلمة الخبيثة) is the opposite of the "good word." Therefore, they differed regarding it, each group saying about it the opposite of what they said about the good word. Similarly, they differed regarding what is meant by the "bad tree" (الشجرة الخبيثة). It was said: it is the colocynth; it was said: the dodder (الكشوث), which is a plant that winds around thorns and trees, having no root in the ground and no leaves; it was said: the garlic plant; it was said: the thorn tree; it was said: algae; it was said: the truffle; and it was said: every tree that does not produce good fruit.

You have already seen the state of these differences in the previous verse, and you have also seen what contemplation yields regarding the meaning of the "good word" and what it was likened to. Its opposite applies to the "bad word" and what it was likened to, letter for letter. It is the word of polytheism (كلمة الشرك), likened to a hypothetical bad tree uprooted from the surface of the earth, having no steady root and no stability. Since it is bad, it has no effect except harm and evil.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ** — "Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word..." (14:27) to the end of the verse. It is apparent that "with the word" (بالقول) relates to His saying "fortifies" (يُثَبِّتُ), not to His saying "believed" (آمَنُوا). The preposition "with" (بـ) denotes instrumentality or causality, not transitivity. Furthermore, His saying **فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ** (in the life of this world and in the Hereafter) also relates to His saying "fortifies," not to "firm" (الثَّابِتِ).

Thus, the meaning returns to this: those who believed, if they remain firm in their faith and steadfast, Allah fortifies them in it in the worldly life and the hereafter. Were it not for His fortification, the Exalted, their own firmness would avail them nothing, nor would they benefit from any of its fruits. To Him, the Exalted, all matters return. Thus, His saying **يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ** in the chapter of guidance (الهداية) balances His saying **فَلَمَّا زاغُوا أَزاغَ اللهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ** — "So when they deviated, Allah made their hearts deviate" (61:5) in the chapter of misguidance (الإضلال).

However, there is a difference between the two chapters: guidance begins from Allah, the Glorified, and the servant's being guided follows from it; whereas misguidance begins from the servant by his own evil choice, so Allah recompenses him with misguidance for his misguidance, as He said: **وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ** — "and He does not mislead by it any except the transgressors" (2:26). Quranic verses are numerous in stating that guidance is from Allah, the Glorified, and no other has any part in it.

To clarify the station: Allah, the Glorified, created man upon a sound primordial nature (فطرة سليمة) in which He established the knowledge of His Lordship and inspired it with its vice and its godwariness. This is a primary, primordial guidance (هداية فطرية أولية). Then He supported it with the religious call (الدعوة الدينية) undertaken by His prophets and messengers.

Then, if man proceeds according to the soundness of his primordial nature and yearns for knowledge and righteous deeds, Allah guides him, and the servant attains faith through His guidance, the Exalted. As for his proceeding according to the soundness of the primordial nature, if it is called "being guided," it is a guidance branching from the primordial soundness, if that itself is called guidance.

But if man deviates from the path of the primordial nature by his evil choice, remains ignorant of the station of his Lord, clings to the earth, follows passion, and opposes the truth, then this is a misguidance from him not preceded by a misguiding from Allah—far be He, the Glorified, from that. However, it is followed by a misguiding from the path as a recompense, and a fortification of him in what he is upon by cutting off His mercy and stripping him of success (التوفيق). This is a misguiding preceded by a misguidance from his own self by his evil choice, and a deviation of him following a deviation from him.

From here, the reason for the difference in context in the two verses is clear. As for His saying **فَلَمَّا زاغُوا أَزاغَ اللهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ** (So when they deviated, Allah made their hearts deviate), He assumed a deviation from them first, then a deviation from Him, the Exalted. As for His saying **يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ** (Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word), He assumed a faith firm upon the fortification, which in itself necessitates a guidance from Him and a being guided from them. Then He added to that the "firm word"—which is their firmness and steadfastness by their good choice upon what they believed in, which is their act—so Allah follows it with their fortification because of that firm word and their preservation from deviation and slipping. He thereby repels from them the dangers of life in the worldly life and the hereafter. This is a guidance from Him, the Exalted, not preceded by a being guided from their own selves that it would be linked to. Understand this well.

In any case, this fortification, in view of the parable, is equivalent to the strengthening of the good tree by the steadiness of its root in the earth. When the root of the tree is steady, it grows and branches out and produces fruits in every season. The worldly life and the hereafter correspond to "every season" (كُلَّ حِينٍ), for the worldly life and the hereafter encompass all times. This is what the context yields of the meaning of the verse.

It was said: the meaning is that Allah fortifies those who believed and establishes them in His honor and reward because of the firm word that was found from them—namely, the word of faith—because it is firm through proofs and evidence. Thus, what is meant by their fortification is bringing them near to Him and settling them in Paradise, and by the firmness of their word is its support by proof and demonstration. In this, there is a restriction without a restrictor.

It was also said: the meaning is that He fortifies them with empowerment in the earth, victory, conquest, and triumph in the worldly life, and settling in Paradise in the hereafter. This is far from the context.

His saying: **وَيُضِلُّ اللهُ الظَّالِمِينَ** — "and Allah leads the wrongdoers astray" (14:27). The apparent contrast between the "wrongdoers" (الظالمين) and "those who have faith" in the previous sentence is that what is meant by them are the people of disbelief in Allah and His signs. Furthermore, He, the Exalted, has explained "the wrongdoers" in an absolute sense in some of His speech with what is close to this, as He said: **أَنْ لَعْنَةُ اللهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَيَبْغُونَها عِوَجاً وَهُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ كافِرُونَ** — "‘The curse of Allah be on the wrongdoers, who bar [others] from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked, and who disbelieve in the Hereafter’" (7:44-45).

The sentence is like the conclusion extracted from the second parable: **وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ اجْتُثَّتْ مِنْ فَوْقِ الْأَرْضِ ما لَها مِنْ قَرارٍ** (And the parable of a bad word is that of a bad tree: uprooted from the ground, it has no stability). The meaning is that Allah leads the people of disbelief astray by depriving them of the path of guidance; thus, they are not guided to a happy life in the worldly life, nor to an enduring blessing and pleasure from Allah in the hereafter. Nothing is found with them—if their hearts were uncovered—except doubt, hesitation, anxiety, agitation, sorrow, regret, and grief.

His saying: **وَيَفْعَلُ اللهُ ما يَشاءُ** — "and Allah does whatever He wishes" (14:27). That is, the fortification of these and the misguiding of those proceeds according to what His will necessitates; there is no preventer for Him nor any repeller. There is no barrier between His will and His act.

From this, it appears that Allah, the Exalted, has willed the fortification of these and the misguiding of those, and He is their Doer inevitably. Thus, the felicity of the believer and the wretchedness of the disbeliever are among the "decreed judgments" (القضاء المحتوم), as has been mentioned in the narrations.

The occurrence of the Name of Majesty (لفظ الجلالة) in His saying **وَيُضِلُّ اللهُ** (and Allah leads astray) and His saying **وَيَفْعَلُ اللهُ** (and Allah does) is the use of the manifest noun in place of the pronoun, and it indicates the magnitude of the matter and the awe of the station, as has been said.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دارَ الْبَوارِ** — "Have you not regarded those who substituted ungratefulness for Allah’s blessing, and settled their people in the abode of ruin?" (14:28). It is said in *al-Majma'*: "Settling" (الإحلال) is placing a thing in a location (محل), either by proximity if it is of the category of bodies, or by inherence if it is of the category of accidents. "Ruin" (البوار) is destruction (الهلاك). It is said: "the thing was ruined" (بار الشيء يبور بورا) when it perished. A man is "ruined" (بور) meaning perished, and a people are "ruined" (بور) as well. End quote.

Al-Raghib said: "Ruin" (البوار) is excessive stagnation (فرط الكساد). Since excessive stagnation leads to corruption—as it is said: "it stagnated until it corrupted"—ruin was used to express destruction. It is said: "the thing was ruined" (بار الشيء يبور بورا وبؤرا). Allah, the All-Mighty and Majestic, said: **تِجارَةً لَنْ تَبُورَ** — "a commerce that will never perish" (35:29). End quote.

The verse mentions the state of the imams of disbelief (أئمة الكفر) and the leaders of misguidance (رؤساء الضلال) in their wrongdoing and their ingratitude toward the blessing of Allah, the Glorified, which surrounded them from every side, instead of thanking Him and believing in their Lord. He had previously mentioned how He, the Exalted, created the heavens and the earth out of His Self-Sufficiency—which is a blessing—then He mentioned the word of truth to which He calls and its steady, good effects—which is a blessing.

The verse is absolute; there is no evidence for restricting it to the disbelievers of Mecca or the disbelievers of Quraysh, even if the address in it is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The presence of phrases at its end like **قُلْ تَمَتَّعُوا فَإِنَّ مَصِيرَكُمْ إِلَى النَّارِ** (Say, ‘Enjoy! Indeed your destination is the Fire’) does not necessitate a restriction in the verse, given the absolute nature of its content and its inclusion of the tyrants (الطواغيت) of the nations and what they did to their peoples.

Thus, His saying **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً** (Have you not regarded those who substituted ungratefulness for Allah’s blessing) mentions the state of the imams of disbelief and leaders of misguidance from the past nations and from this nation. The evidence for its restriction to them is His saying **وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دارَ الْبَوارِ** (and settled their people in the abode of ruin), which suggests their word was influential and they were obeyed among their people; thus, they are the imams and leaders.

What is meant by their "substituting ungratefulness for Allah’s blessing" is substituting disbelief (كفراً) for the gratitude (شكر) for His blessing that was obligatory upon them. Thus, in the sentence, there is a deleted "annexed noun" (مضاف محذوف), the estimation being: "they substituted disbelief for the gratitude for Allah's blessing." It is also possible that what is meant is the substitution of the blessing itself with disbelief by a type of metaphor (تجوز). Similar to the verse in this regard is the saying of the Exalted: **وَتَجْعَلُونَ رِزْقَكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ تُكَذِّبُونَ** — "and make your denial your [only] share [of it]?" (56:82).

The mention of their settling their people in the abode of ruin necessitates their settling themselves in it as well, because they are imams of misguidance who went astray and then led others astray, and the consequence is the consequence of misguidance. Similar to the verse in this meaning is His saying regarding Pharaoh: **يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ النَّارَ** — "He will lead his people on the Day of Resurrection and conduct them into the Fire" (11:98).

The meaning is: Have you not looked at the imams and leaders from the past nations and from your nation who substituted disbelief for the gratitude for Allah's blessing, and their people followed them, so they settled and settled their people in the abode of destruction—which is wretchedness and the Fire.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَها وَبِئْسَ الْقَرارُ** — "Hell, which they shall enter—and it is an evil abiding place" (14:29). This is an exposition (بيان) of the "abode of ruin." The possibility raised by some—that "Hell" (جَهَنَّمَ) is in the accusative due to "distraction" (الاشتغال), the estimation being "they shall enter Hell, they shall enter it," and the sentence being a new start (مستأنفة)—is devoid of merit because the accusative is less preferred and there is no subtle point necessitating a new start.

From here, the invalidity of the view of those who said the verses are Medinan and that what is meant by "those who disbelieved" are the grandees of Mecca and the chiefs of Quraysh who gathered the masses against the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and fought him at Badr, so they were killed and settled their people in the abode of ruin, becomes clear.

This is because you have learned from the meaning of the verse that it is absolute, and there is no reason to restrict it to the slain of Badr among the disbelievers at all. Rather, the verse includes every imam of misguidance who settled his people in the abode of ruin, from those who preceded and those who followed. What is meant by "settling in the abode of ruin" is establishing them in the wretchedness of the Fire, even if they have not yet been killed, died, or entered the Fire.

Furthermore, the apparent meaning of the following verse—**وَجَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ أَنْداداً لِيُضِلُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ قُلْ تَمَتَّعُوا فَإِنَّ مَصِيرَكُمْ إِلَى النَّارِ** (They set up equals to Allah to lead [people] astray from His way. Say, ‘Enjoy! Indeed your destination is the Fire’)—is that the plural pronoun returns to "those who disbelieved" mentioned in this verse. This would necessitate that the address "Say, ‘Enjoy!’" be an address to those remaining among them—namely, those who embraced Islam on the day of the Conquest—which would be a threat of certain, realized wretchedness without exception.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَجَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ أَنْداداً لِيُضِلُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ قُلْ تَمَتَّعُوا فَإِنَّ مَصِيرَكُمْ إِلَى النَّارِ** — "They set up equals to Allah to lead [people] astray from His way. Say, ‘Enjoy! Indeed your destination is the Fire’" (14:30). "Equals" (الأنداد) is the plural of "equal" (ند), which is a peer (المثل). They are the deities they took as gods besides Allah from among the angels, jinn, and humans.

They only made them "equals" while confessing that they were created by Allah, the Glorified, from the perspective that they named them gods and lords, attributed the management of the world's affairs to them, and then worshipped them out of fear and hope. This was despite the fact that the command and the creation belong entirely to Allah—as their primordial nature confessed and as Allah supported with the signs and proofs He inspired in His prophets and messengers indicating His oneness.

Thus, they were acting with insight regarding the matter of monotheism (التوحيد); they did not take equals out of heedlessness or error. Rather, they did so seeking the fleeting goods of the worldly life and to enslave people and exploit them by leading them astray from the way of Allah. Therefore, He explained the reason for their taking equals with His saying: **لِيُضِلُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ** (to lead [people] astray from His way). Then He commanded the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to threaten them with the Fire, to which is their return and they have no return other than it, saying: **قُلْ تَمَتَّعُوا فَإِنَّ مَصِيرَكُمْ إِلَى النَّارِ** (Say, ‘Enjoy! Indeed your destination is the Fire’).

The natural course of the speech would have been to say to them: "Take equals" or "Lead astray from the way of Allah, for your destination is the Fire." However, it was substituted with His saying **تَمَتَّعُوا** (Enjoy!) to explicitly state their corrupt purpose which they were hiding, so as to be more eloquent in their exposure.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قُلْ لِعِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَيُنْفِقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ سِرًّا وَعَلانِيَةً مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ يَوْمٌ لا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَلا خِلالٌ** — "Say to My servants who have faith that they should maintain the prayer and spend out of what We have provided them, secretly and openly, before there comes a day in which there will be no bargaining, nor friendship" (14:31). When He threatened them through the tongue of His Messenger with the punishment of the Day of Resurrection for leading people astray from His way, He commanded him to order His servants who believed to adhere to His way before the Day of Resurrection comes, when they will not be able to rectify what they missed of felicity through any of the means circulating among them for that purpose. These means return to one of two things: either exchange by giving something and taking its equivalent—which is "bargaining" (البيع) in its more general sense—or "friendship" (الخلة) and love. None of these means have any effect on a Day purely for reckoning and recompense; for that is the nature of the Day of Resurrection and nothing else.

From here, it appears that His saying **يُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَيُنْفِقُوا** (they should maintain the prayer and spend) is an exposition of the "way of Allah" (سبيل الله). He sufficed with these two pillars by which all other religious duties are joined—those that rectify man's worldly life in what is between him and his Lord, and what is between him and the other members of his species.

His saying **يُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَيُنْفِقُوا** (they should maintain the prayer and spend), etc., are in the jussive case (مجزومان) because they occur as the response to the command (جواب الأمر). The "content of the saying" (مقول القول) is deleted because the two verbs indicate it. The estimation is: "Say: 'Maintain the prayer and spend (etc.)'; they will maintain the prayer and spend (etc.)."

The objection—that the verb in the jussive as a response to a command must be a necessary consequence of it, and it does not follow from the command to pray and spend that they will obey it—[is addressed elsewhere].

...is invalid, for it is necessary that the response be something required by the command in some respect; and His commanding His believing servants—who are indeed believing servants—is something that necessitates obedience without any doubt.

The spending (الإنفاق) mentioned in the verse refers to spending in the way of Allah in a general sense, as the Surah is Meccan and the verse of zakat (الزكاة) — *the obligatory alms-tax* — had not yet been revealed. The meaning of spending "secretly and openly" is that the spending should proceed according to what is necessitated by true religious etiquette (الأدب الديني); thus, one keeps it secret where secrecy is appropriate and makes it public where publicity is appropriate. What is sought by this, in any case, is to perform that which rectifies what is liable to corruption and straightens the affairs of the Muslim community.

The negation of "friendship" (المخالة) in this verse does not contradict His saying, the Exalted: **الْأَخِلَّاءُ يَوْمَئِذٍ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ إِلَّا الْمُتَّقِينَ** "On that day friends will be one another’s enemies, except for the God-conscious" (43:67). This is because the relationship between the two verses is one of absolute generality and specificity (العموم والخصوص المطلق); thus, this verse is specified by that one. The meaning derived from both verses is that every friendship (خلة) not based on godwariness (التقوى) — *the state of being conscious of Allah's presence and boundaries* — will be severed on the Day of Resurrection. As for the friendship based on it, which is friendship for the sake of Allah's essence, it remains firm and beneficial. Thus, the absolute negation of friendship followed by the affirmation of some of it in the two verses is similar to the absolute negation of intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — in His saying: **وَلا خُلَّةٌ وَلا شَفاعَةٌ** "...neither shall there be any friendship or intercession" (2:254), followed by its affirmation for those who have Allah's permission, as in His saying: **إِلَّا مَنْ شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ** "...excepting such as witness to the truth and have knowledge" (43:86).

As for what has been said regarding the negation of contradiction—that the "friendship" (الخلال) in the negating verse refers to friendship based on worldly means to compensate for what was missed, unlike the affirming verse; or that the negated friendship is that which follows natural inclination and the soul's desire, unlike the affirmed friendship which is for the sake of Allah's essence—both views essentially return to what we have mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **اللهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ** "It is Allah who created the heavens and the earth..." (14:32) etc. When the Glorified mentioned that they set up equals (أندادا) to Allah to mislead people from the way of Allah and threatened them for it, He presented in this verse, through to the end of the next three verses, the proof for the exclusivity of Lordship (الربوبية) to Himself, the Exalted and Sanctified. This is established through the exclusivity of general governance (التدبير العام) to Him, manifested in the order of creation, the sending down of water, the bringing forth of sustenance, and the subjugation (تسخير) of the seas—the ships (الفلك)—the rivers, the sun, the moon, the night, and the day.

He pointed out at the end of the verses that these, and countless others, are blessings (نعمة) from Him, the Exalted, to man. This is because the discourse in this Surah—as previously indicated—proceeds in the light of the two Names: the All-Mighty (العزيز), the All-Laudable (الحميد).

Thus, His saying: **اللهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ** "It is Allah who created..." is in the sense of our saying: "He alone is the Lord, to the exclusion of those you have set up as equals to Him."

And His saying: **وَأَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَخْرَجَ بِهِ** "...and sent down water from the sky, and with it brought forth..." (14:32). What is meant by "the sky" (السماء) is the direction of height, which is its linguistic meaning. The water descending from it is the rain, for to it returns all the water on earth by which living things—plants and animals—survive.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْفُلْكَ لِتَجْرِيَ فِي الْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِ وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْأَنْهارَ** "...and He disposed the ships for you to sail on the sea by His command, and He disposed the rivers for you" (14:32). The subjugation (تسخير) of the ships for people means making them such that they benefit them in their objectives, such as transporting themselves and their cargo, without sinking in the water or being unable to move.

As for the view of some that their "subjugation" means giving people the power to manufacture and use them by inspiring them with the method—this is far-fetched. For the apparent meaning of subjugating something for man is to act upon that thing by making it conform to the benefits he seeks, not to act upon man himself through inspiration and the like.

The natural flow of the speech would have been to say: "He disposed the sea for you so that the ships may sail in it by His command, and He disposed the rivers for you." However, it was reversed, and it was said: **وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْفُلْكَ لِتَجْرِيَ فِي الْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِ** "...and He disposed the ships for you to sail on the sea by His command," because the ship is among the clearest of maritime blessings, even if His blessings are not limited to it. Perhaps this is the reason for the reversal, as the context is one of enumerating blessings, and the blessing in the ship is clearer, even if the blessing in the sea is greater.

The attribution of their sailing in the sea to His command (أمره), the Exalted, despite it being attributed to active natural causes (الأسباب الطبيعية) such as wind, steam, and other factors, is because He, the Exalted, is the encompassing Cause (السبب المحيط) to whom every cause ultimately returns.

And His saying: **وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْأَنْهارَ** "...and He disposed the rivers for you," which are the flowing waters in various regions of the earth. Their subjugation means making them subservient (تدليلها) such that man benefits from them for drinking, washing, removing filth, and other uses, and by which the animals and plants subjugated to him survive.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ دائِبَيْنِ وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ** "And He disposed the sun and the moon for you, constant [in their courses], and He disposed the night and the day for you" (14:33). Al-Raghib said: "The word *da'b* (الدأب) means the continuation of travel... He, the Exalted, said: **دائِبَيْنِ** 'constant [in their courses]'. *Al-da'b* also means a habit that continues constantly in one state, as He, the Exalted, said: **كَدَأْبِ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ** 'Like the custom of Pharaoh’s clan' (8:52), meaning like their habit which they continued upon." [End of quote]. The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَآتاكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ ما سَأَلْتُمُوهُ وَإِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ لا تُحْصُوها إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ** "And He gave you all that you asked Him. If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them. Indeed man is most unjust and ungrateful" (14:34). Asking (السؤال) is requesting (الطلب), but it differs in that asking is only from one who possesses intellect, while requesting is more general. Man only becomes aware of asking due to the need that calls for it; thus, it becomes apparent to him to seek the removal of the need that has befallen him. The usual means for this is speech, so he employs it; sometimes he employs a gesture or writing, and it is truly called "asking" without metaphor.

Since Allah, the Glorified, is the One who removes the need of every needy being besides Him, and nothing in its existence or survival attaches itself to anything except the hem of His bounty and generosity—whether it acknowledges Him or denies Him—and He, the Exalted, knows them and their outward and inward needs better than they know themselves, every being besides Him is a devotee at the door of His bounty, asking Him to remove the need that has befallen it, whether He gives or withholds, and whether He answers all that was asked or some of it.

This is the true nature and reality of asking (السؤال), which is exclusive to Him, the Exalted, and does not extend to anyone else. Some asking is verbal—as mentioned—by which Allah, the Glorified, may be asked, or others may be asked. Thus, He, the Exalted, is the One asked; everything asks Him through the reality of asking, and some people among the believers ask Him through verbal asking.

This is regarding the asking; as for the giving (الإيتاء), it has been mentioned generally without being restricted by an exception or the like, which indicates that there is no asking except that there is a giving with it. This is a contextual clue that the address is to the species (النوع), as is also supported by the conclusion: **إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ** "Indeed man is most unjust and ungrateful."

The meaning is that the human species, in its specific nature, has not needed any blessing except that Allah has fulfilled its need, either entirely or partially, even if an individual (الفرد) might need and ask but his need is not granted.

This meaning is supported by His saying, the Exalted: **أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ** "I answer the solicitor’s invocation when he delivers it to Me" (2:186). It was mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of that verse that He, the Exalted, does not reject the supplication of one who calls upon Him, unless it is not a true supplication, or it is a supplication but not to Him, but rather to someone else. An individual human might have his tongue disagree with his heart or speak nonsense in his supplication, but the species, in its specific nature, knows neither vanity nor hypocrisy, and knows no Lord other than Him, the Glorified. Whenever a need touches it, it asks Him in reality, and asks none but its Lord; thus, all its supplications are answered, its requests are granted, and its needs are fulfilled.

It has become clear from what preceded that the word "from" (مِنْ) in His saying: **مِنْ كُلِّ ما سَأَلْتُمُوهُ** "of all that you asked Him" is for beginning (ابتدائية), indicating that what Allah gives is taken from what they asked, whether it was all they asked, as in some cases, or some of it, as in others. If it were partitive (تبعيضية), it would imply that He gives in every asking only a part of what was asked, and the reality is otherwise. Similarly, if it were said: "And He gave you all that you asked Him," it would imply giving everything, which is not the case. If it were said: "of what you asked Him," it would imply that it is possible for some supplications not to be answered and some requests to be rejected from the root, and the verse—being in the context of reminding of favors—rejects that.

In summary, the meaning of the verse is that Allah the Exalted gave the human species what it asked; there is no need among its needs except that He removed all or some of it according to what His profound wisdom necessitates.

It has been said that the implied meaning is: "And He gave you of all that you asked Him and what you did not ask Him," but this is based on the assumption that "asking" refers only to verbal asking, and the opposite has already been established, and the context of the verse does not support it.

And His saying: **وَإِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ لا تُحْصُوها** "If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them" (14:34). Al-Raghib said: "*Al-ihsa'* (الإحصاء) is obtaining by number... it is derived from the word *al-hasa* (الحصى) — *pebbles* — and its use in this sense is because they used to rely on them for counting as we rely on fingers." [End of quote].

The sentence contains an indication that the blessings exceed the power of enumeration (الإحصاء), and its necessary consequence is that the human needs which Allah has removed through His blessings are beyond man's ability to count.

And how can His blessings, the Exalted, be counted when the world of existence (عالم الوجود) with all its parts and the attributes and states attached to them are interconnected and organized, some benefiting others and some depending on others? Thus, everything is a blessing (نعمة) in relation to everything else, and this is a matter that no enumeration can encompass.

Perhaps that is the secret behind the singular form of "blessing" in His saying: **نِعْمَتَ اللهِ** "Allah's blessing," for the truth is that there is nothing but the blessing; thus, there is no need to emphasize it with the plural to indicate multiplicity. What is meant by "blessing" is the genus of what is bestowed (جنس المنعم), thus conveying what the plural conveys.

And His saying: **إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ** "Indeed man is most unjust and ungrateful," meaning very ungrateful (كثير الكفران), oppressing himself by not thanking Allah for the blessing and being ungrateful for it, which leads him to ruin and loss; or very unjust (كثير الظلم) toward Allah's blessings by not thanking them and being ungrateful for them. The sentence is an explanatory resumption (استئناف بياني) by which what was understood from the previous explanation is emphasized. For one who stands upon what has been explained regarding the state of His blessings, the Exalted, and what He has given man of all that he asked of them, will not doubt that man—being naturally heedless of them—is unjust to himself and ungrateful for the blessing of his Lord.

## (Commentary on Narrations)
