In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, it is narrated by al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, al-Bazzar, Abu Ya'la, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Hibban, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and Ibn Mardawayh, from Anas, who said: "A tray of fresh dates was brought to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and he said: **مَثَلاً كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ** '...a parable: a good word is like a good tree...' until he reached **تُؤْتِي أُكُلَها كُلَّ حِينٍ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّها** 'It gives its produce every season by the leave of its Lord' (14:24-25). He said: 'It is the date palm.' **وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ** 'And the parable of a bad word is a bad tree...' until he reached **ما لَها مِنْ قَرارٍ** '...having no stability' (14:26). He said: 'It is the colocynth (الحنظلة).'"

I say: That the "good tree" is the date palm is narrated in several reports from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and they do not indicate more than the application (انطباق) of the parable to it. The end of the narration contradicts the following one.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "A group of the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sat and mentioned this verse: **اجْتُثَّتْ مِنْ فَوْقِ الْأَرْضِ ما لَها مِنْ قَرارٍ** '...uprooted from the ground, having no stability' (14:26). They said: 'O Messenger of Allah, we see it as the truffle (الكمأة).' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'The truffle is from the manna (المن), and its water is a cure for the eye; and the *ajwah* date is from Paradise, and it is a cure for poison.'"

I say: The same applies to the colocynth, for it has important medicinal properties.

In the same source, al-Bayhaqi narrated in his *Sunan* from Ali (عليه‌السلام) who said: "The 'season' (الحين) is six months."

I say: The discussion regarding this is like the discussion regarding the previous one.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission to 'Amr ibn Hurayth, who said: "I asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: **كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ أَصْلُها ثابِتٌ وَفَرْعُها فِي السَّماءِ** '...like a good tree: its roots are steady and its branches are in the sky' (14:24). He said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is its root, and the Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام) is its branch, and the Imams from their progeny are its boughs, and the knowledge of the Imams is its fruit, and their believing followers (الشيعة) are its leaves. Is there any excess in this?' I said: 'No, by Allah.' He said: 'By Allah, indeed when a believer is born, a leaf sprouts on it, and when a believer dies, a leaf falls from it.'"

I say: The narration is based on the "good word" (الكلمة الطيبة) being the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The term "word" (الكلمة) has been applied in His speech to a human, as in His saying: **بِكَلِمَةٍ مِنْهُ اسْمُهُ الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ** "...with a word from Him whose name is Messiah, Jesus son of Mary" (3:45). Nevertheless, the narration is by way of application (التطبيق). Evidence for this is the variation in narrations regarding the manner of application: in some, the root is the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), the branch is Ali (عليه‌السلام), the boughs are the Imams (عليه‌السلام), the fruit is their knowledge, and the leaves are the followers (الشيعة), as in this narration. In others, the tree is the Messenger of Allah, its branch is Ali, the bough is Fatimah, its fruit is her children, and its leaves are our followers, as narrated by al-Saduq from Jabir from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him). In yet others, the Prophet and the Imams are the steady root, and the branch is the mastership (الولاية) for whoever enters into it, as in *al-Kafi* from Muhammad al-Halabi from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him).

In *al-Majma'*, Abu al-Jarud narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): "This—meaning His saying: **كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ** '...like a bad tree...' etc.—is a parable for the Umayyads (بني أمية)."

In the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi, from 'Abd al-Rahman ibn Salim al-Ashal from his father from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): **ضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ** "Allah draws a parable: a good word is like a good tree..." the two verses. He said: "This is a parable Allah drew for the People of the House (أهل بيت) of His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and for those who show enmity toward them, it is the parable of **كَلِمَة خَبِيثَة كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ ـ اجْتُثَّتْ مِنْ فَوْقِ الْأَرْضِ ما لَها مِنْ قَرارٍ** '...a bad word is a bad tree: uprooted from the ground, having no stability.'"

I say: Al-Alusi said in the *Tafsir* of *Ruh al-Ma'ani*, verbatim: "The Imamiyyah—and you know their state—narrated from Abu Ja'far (may Allah be pleased with him) the interpretation of it—meaning the bad tree—as the Umayyads, and the interpretation of the good tree as the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), Ali (may Allah ennoble his face), Fatimah (may Allah be pleased with her), and what was born of them. In some narrations of the Sunnis, there is that which troubles the interpretation of the bad tree as the Umayyads. Ibn Mardawayh narrated from 'Adi ibn Hatim who said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: Indeed Allah the Exalted turned the servants over, inside and out, and the best of His servants were the Arabs; and He turned the Arabs over, inside and out, and the best of the Arabs were Quraysh; and it is the blessed tree of which Allah the Exalted said in His Book: **مَثَلاً كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ** "a parable: a good word is like a good tree," because the Umayyads are from Quraysh.'" [End of the relevant portion].

This is strange, for the fact that a nation or a group is blessed according to their nature does not necessitate that all the branches branching from it are likewise. The narration, assuming its acceptance, only indicates that Quraysh is a blessed tree. As for all the branches branching from it being blessed and good—such as the Banu 'Abd al-Dar, for example—or every individual among them being so—such as Abu Jahl and Abu Lahab—certainly not. What necessary connection is there between a tree being blessed and good in its root, and some of its branches that separated from it and grew in a corrupt growth being blessed and good?

Ibn Mardawayh narrated this from 'Aishah: that she said to Marwan ibn al-Hakam: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying to your father and your grandfather: 'Indeed you are the cursed tree (الشجرة الملعونة) in the Quran.'"

The authors of the *Tafsir* works, such as al-Tabari and others, narrated from Sahl ibn Sa'd, 'Abdullah ibn 'Umar, Ya'la ibn Murrah, al-Husayn ibn Ali, and Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib: that they are the ones regarding whom His saying, the Exalted, was revealed: **وَما جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْناكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ ـ وَالشَّجَرَةَ الْمَلْعُونَةَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ** "...We did not make the vision We showed you except as a tribulation for the people and the tree cursed in the Quran..." (17:60). The wording of Sa'd is: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saw the children of so-and-so leaping upon his pulpit like the leaping of monkeys, and that distressed him, so he did not laugh fully until he died, and Allah revealed: **وَما جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا** '...We did not make the vision...'"

The narration from 'Umar and from Ali will follow in the commentary of His saying: **الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً** "...those who changed Allah’s blessing for ungratefulness..." (14:28), that they are the two most wicked groups of Quraysh: the Banu al-Mughirah and the Banu Umayyah.

In the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi, from Safwan ibn Mihran from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Satan comes to a man from our friends (أوليائنا) at the time of his death, coming to him from his right and his left to turn him away from what he is upon, but Allah refuses that. Thus Allah said: **يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ** 'Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter' (14:27)."

In the same source, from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon them), who said: "When a man is placed in his grave, two angels come to him—an angel from his right and an angel from his left—and Satan is stood before him, his eyes like copper. It is said to him: 'What do you say about this man who came out from among you, claiming to be the Messenger of Allah?' He becomes terrified by that. If he is a believer, he says: 'Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah.' It is said at that: 'Sleep a sleep in which there is no dream,' and his grave is expanded for him nine cubits, and he sees his seat in Paradise. This is the saying of Allah: **يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا** 'Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world.' If he is a disbeliever, they say: 'Who is this man who was among you, saying that he is the Messenger of Allah?' He says: 'I do not know.' Then he is left between him and Satan."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, it is narrated by al-Tayalisi, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh, from al-Bara' ibn 'Azib, that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "When the Muslim is questioned in the grave, he bears witness that there is no god but Allah and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah. That is His saying, the Glorified: **يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ ـ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ** 'Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter' (14:27)."

In the same source, al-Tabarani in *al-Awsat* and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying regarding this verse: **يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ ـ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ** 'Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter'—he said: 'In the Hereafter is the grave.'"

I say: There are many narrations from the paths of the Shi'a and the Sunnis regarding the details of the questioning in the grave, the coming of the two angels Munkar and Nakir, and the firmness of the believer and the straying of the disbeliever at that time, and many of them have relied upon this verse.

Their apparent meaning is that what is meant by "the Hereafter" (الآخرة) is the grave and the world of death. Perhaps this is based on the apparent meaning of "fortifying" (التثبيت), for the apparent meaning of giving firmness is that it be in a place where slipping and confusion are possible. This is only conceivable in other than the Day of Resurrection, in which there is nothing but the recompense for deeds. However, considering that everything firm in existence is only firm through Allah, the Glorified—whether it is something liable to cessation or not—there is no difference between the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* — and the Resurrection in that the believer is firm through the fortification of Allah, the Glorified. It is better to take the narrations as being of the nature of application (التطبيق).

In the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi, from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah, who said: "The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said regarding His saying: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً** 'Have you not regarded those who changed Allah’s blessing for ungratefulness...' (14:28): 'We are the blessing of Allah which Allah bestowed upon the servants.'"

I say: This is from the category of flow and application (الجري والتطبيق).

In the same source, from Mi'sam al-Musrif from Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: **وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دارَ الْبَوارِ** "...and landed their people in the abode of ruin?" (14:28). He said: "They are the two most wicked groups of Quraysh: the Banu Umayyah and the Banu al-Mughirah." I say: It was also narrated in *al-Burhan* from Ibn Shahr Ashub from Abu al-Tufayl from him (عليه‌السلام).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani in *al-Awsat*, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated through various paths from Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding His saying: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً** "Have you not regarded those who changed Allah’s blessing for ungratefulness..." (14:28). He said: "They are the two most wicked groups of Quraysh: the Banu Umayyah and the Banu al-Mughirah. As for the Banu al-Mughirah, Allah cut off their roots on the day of Badr; as for the Banu Umayyah, they were given enjoyment for a time."

I say: This is narrated from 'Umar as will follow.

In the same source, al-Bukhari in his *Tarikh*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from 'Umar ibn al-Khattab regarding His saying: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً** "Have you not regarded those who changed Allah’s blessing for ungratefulness..." (14:28). He said: "They are the two most wicked groups of Quraysh: the Banu al-Mughirah and the Banu Umayyah. As for the Banu al-Mughirah, you were spared from them on the day of Badr; as for the Banu Umayyah, they were given enjoyment for a time."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn 'Abbas: that he said to 'Umar: "O Commander of the Faithful, this verse: **الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً** '...those who changed Allah’s blessing for ungratefulness...'?" He said: "They are the two most wicked groups of Quraysh: my maternal uncles and your paternal uncles. As for my maternal uncles, Allah eradicated them on the day of Badr; as for your paternal uncles, Allah gave them respite for a time."

In the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi, from Dharih from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I heard him saying: Ibn al-Kawwa' came to the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) and asked him about the saying of Allah: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا** 'Have you not regarded those who changed...' the verse. He said: 'That is Quraysh; they changed Allah's blessing for ungratefulness and denied His Prophet on the day of Badr.'"

I say: The variation in application (التطبيق) in his (عليه‌السلام) speech is evidence that it is from the category of explaining the verse's applicability, not from the category of the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول).

In *al-Kafi*, from Ali ibn Muhammad from some of his companions in a marfu' (مرفوع) — *attributed to the Imam* — narration, he said: "When Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) read this verse: **وَإِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ لا تُحْصُوها** 'If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them' (14:34), he would say: 'Glory be to Him who did not place in anyone any knowledge of His blessings except the knowledge of falling short in knowing them, just as He did not place in anyone any knowledge of perceiving Him more than the knowledge that he cannot perceive Him. Thus, He, the Majestic and Exalted, thanked the knowledge of the knowers regarding their falling short in knowing how to thank Him, and He made their knowledge of falling short a form of thanks; just as He knew the knowledge of the knowers that they cannot perceive Him, and He made that knowledge.'" [The narration continues].
